coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of magnetic carbon dot drug complex for targeting to nucleus in liver cancer therapy and fluorescence/magnetic resonance dual-modal imaging

coredata.dc:description: To enhance the targeting and monitoring of liver cancer therapy, this study developed a novel magnetic carbon dot (Gd-CDs) drug complex for targeting to nucleus with dual-modal fluorescence/magnetic resonance imaging (FL/MRI) capabilities for precise diagnosis and efficient treatment of liver cancer. Gd-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid, neutral red, and thiourea as carbon sources and gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate as the gadolinium source. A nucleus localization sequence (NLS) and the anti-tumor drug doxorubicin (DOX) were sequentially grafted onto their surface to construct the magnetic carbon dot drug complex (Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX) for targeting to nucleus. The complex exhibited optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 500 and 601 nm, respectively, and a longitudinal relaxivity of 8.25 mM−1 s−1 with a drug loading efficiency of 49.3 %. It also showed low toxicity, could be excreted from mice within 24 h. After incubation with liver cancer cells for 24 h, the cell viability was reduced to around 55 % using 100 μg/mL of the complex, which was superior to free DOX. Furthermore, the tumor volume treated with this complex was significantly lower than that of the free DOX group. This study provides a new way for efficient targeting treatment and monitoring of liver cancer by Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To enhance the targeting and monitoring of liver cancer therapy, this study developed a novel magnetic carbon dot (Gd-CDs) drug complex for targeting to nucleus with dual-modal fluorescence/magnetic resonance imaging (FL/MRI) capabilities for precise diagnosis and efficient treatment of liver cancer. Gd-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid, neutral red, and thiourea as carbon sources and gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate as the gadolinium source. A nucleus localization sequence (NLS) and the anti-tumor drug doxorubicin (DOX) were sequentially grafted onto their surface to construct the magnetic carbon dot drug complex (Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX) for targeting to nucleus. The complex exhibited optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 500 and 601 nm, respectively, and a longitudinal relaxivity of 8.25 mM−1 s−1 with a drug loading efficiency of 49.3 %. It also showed low toxicity, could be excreted from mice within 24 h. After incubation with liver cancer cells for 24 h, the cell viability was reduced to around 55 % using 100 μg/mL of the complex, which was superior to free DOX. Furthermore, the tumor volume treated with this complex was significantly lower than that of the free DOX group. This study provides a new way for efficient targeting treatment and monitoring of liver cancer by Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Liver cancer ranks as the third leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide, representing a significant global health challenge [1]. For patients with liver cancer, timely and effective monitoring and treatment are crucial for improving cure rates and long-term survival significantly. Current clinical monitoring methods for liver cancer primarily rely on single-mode imaging techniques such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), ultrasound imaging, positron emission tomography, and X-ray computed tomography (CT) [2,3]. Although these single-mode imaging techniques offer high resolution for precise visualization of tumor shape and size, they often exhibit limited sensitivity for small tumors, which can hinder the detection of early-stage lesions [4].

In modern medical imaging and therapeutic techniques, dual-modal imaging combines two different imaging modals, provides more comprehensive and precise imaging information and has attracted significant attention in liver cancer monitoring. Reported dual-modal techniques include fluorescence/magnetic resonance imaging (FL/MRI), FL/photoacoustic imaging, ultrasound/photoacoustic imaging, and CT/photoacoustic imaging [5]. Among these, FL/MRI dual-modal imaging is a new type of highly sensitive and high-resolution monitoring technique that can compensate for the shortcomings of traditional tumor monitoring and has great significance for the future diagnosis and treatment of tumor [6,7]. Wang et al. [8] used hollow mesoporous MnO2 as a carrier to load the photosensitizer indocyanine green, obtaining M−HMnO2@ICG, which exhibits blue fluorescence and a relaxivity (r1) of 1.2 mM−1 s−1. They then achieved FL/MRI dual-modal imaging and therapy in HeLa tumor-bearing mice. Lee et al. [9] developed MnCO3-mineralized polydopamine nanoparticles (MnCO3-FPNPs) with an emission wavelength of 560 nm and an r1 of 5.8 mM−1 s−1 for in vivo FL/MRI dual-modal imaging and photothermal therapy in 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice. It can be concluded that most current research cannot simultaneously achieve both high relaxivity and long-wavelength emission. Therefore, the development of novel probes for FL/MRI dual-modal imaging is crucial.

Magnetic carbon dots (CDs), i.e., gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) represent a promising FL/MRI dual-modal molecular probe that leverages the excellent biocompatibility and stable photoluminescence of CDs [10,11], in addition to inheriting the superior magnetic characteristics of gadolinium ions (Gd3+) [12], displaying considerable potential in bioimaging applications. Chen et al. [13] synthesized functionalized Gd-CDs for tracking the proliferation of human mesenchymal stem cells via FL/MRI dual-modal imaging. Du et al. [14] synthesized folic acid-functionalized Gd-CDs using a hydrothermal method, demonstrating effective FL/MRI targeting imaging capabilities for liver cancer. However, the emission wavelengths of most Gd-CDs are currently concentrated in the blue light region, and their relaxivity is not high. Developing Gd-CDs materials with long wavelengths and high relaxivity is therefore one of the current research directions in FL/MRI dual-modal imaging probes.

In addition to their dual-modal FL/MRI imaging capabilities, Gd-CDs can be readily surface-modified, allowing their use as drug delivery carriers. For example, chemotherapeutic drugs doxorubicin (DOX) can be effectively delivered to specific sites within the body, thereby minimizing toxic side effects on normal cells [15]. However, realizing precision diagnosis and therapy for liver cancer requires imparting Gd-CDs with targeting functionality, allowing them to specifically target liver cancer cells. Nucleus localization sequence (NLS) is a commonly used targeting to nucleus molecule. Upon modification of Gd-CDs with NLS, the resulting complex further interacts with nuclear import proteins α/β in the cytoplasm, forming a dimer that can facilitate the transport of the drug complex through nuclear pores to the cell nucleus [16], thereby enabling targeted therapy, enhancing drug efficacy, and decreasing side effects.

This study aims to enhance the efficiency of clinical liver cancer monitoring and treatment by constructing a magnetic carbon dot drug complex for targeting to nucleus for FL/MRI dual-modal imaging and therapy. Initially, Gd-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method, using citric acid, thiourea, and neutral red as carbon sources and gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate as the gadolinium source (as shown in Scheme 1 ). The effects of reaction time, reaction temperature, and the amount of gadolinium source on the emission wavelength and fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of the Gd-CDs were then investigated. The optimal emission wavelength and FLQY Gd-CDs were subsequently modified with a NLS to prepare carbon dot for targeting to nucleus Gd-CDs (Gd-CDs-NLS).

Further, p-hydrazinobenzoic acid (HBA) was conjugated to the surface of Gd-CDs-NLS, introducing abundant hydrazide groups on the surface. DOX was then loaded via hydrazone bond formation to construct a carbon dot drug complex for targeting to nucleus (Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX) for enhancing therapeutic inhibitory effect on liver cancer.

The study will systematically investigate the biocompatibility of the carrier, the dual-modal FL/MRI imaging capability of the complex, and its therapeutic efficacy in liver cancer to provide novel insights into the treatment and monitoring of this disease.

Neutral red was purchased from Tianjin University Chemical Reagent Factory, Tianjin. Citric acid (C6H10O8), gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate (Gd(NO3)3·6H2O), thiourea, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), HBA, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and glacial acetic acid were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. NLS (PKKKRKVG) was purchased from MedChemExpress. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) was purchased from Wuhan Boster Biological Technology Co., Ltd. 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and DOX were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Normal saline was purchased from Shanghai Baxter Medical Products Co., Ltd. Triton X-100 (TX-100) was purchased from Shanghai Yiyan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Human normal liver cells (THLE-2) and human hepatoma cells (HepG2) were purchased from Wuhan Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd. The CCK-8 kit was purchased from Wuhan Elabscience Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Paraformaldehyde (4 %) was purchased from Beishare Biotech Co., Ltd. DAPI nucleus staining solution was purchased from Shanghai Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Hitachi (Japan). Fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) was determined using a Quantaurus-QY Plus spectrometer from Hamamatsu Photonics (Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a JEM-2010 electron microscope from JEOL (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) measurements were conducted using a 5110 inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer from Agilent Technologies (USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a TENSOR27 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer from Bruker (Germany) using the potassium bromide pellet technique. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded using a U-3900 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer from Hitachi (Japan). MRI was performed using a BioSpec 7 T magnetic resonance imaging system from Bruker (Germany). Mouse serum analysis was performed using a fully automated hematology analyzer from Mindray Medical International (China). In vivo fluorescence images in mice were acquired using an IVIS Lumina XRMS fluorescence imaging system from PerkinElmer (USA). Cell co-localization fluorescence images were acquired using an LSM 880 laser confocal microscope from ZEISS (Germany).

Gd-CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. 0.76 g of thiourea, 1.92 g of citric acid, and a specific amount of Gd(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. Separately, 0.015 g of neutral red was dissolved in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. The two solutions were mixed and vigorously sonicated to ensure uniform mixing. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and reacted in a constant-temperature oven for a set period of time. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the solution was transferred to a 50 mL centrifuge tube. The solution was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered using a 0.22 μm disposable filter membrane and a 2 mL disposable syringe to remove large particulate impurities. Next, the ethanol was removed using a rotary evaporator. The resulting solution was then purified for 48 h using a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove residual organic molecules. After dialysis, the Gd-CDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying.

The synthesis conditions of Gd-CDs were further optimized. First, the reaction time and temperature were optimized. The amount of Gd(NO3)3·6H2O was fixed at 0.9 g, and hydrothermal reactions were performed at 100, 140, and 180 °C for 4, 8, and 12 h, separately. Finally, the amount of the gadolinium source was optimized by varying the amount of Gd(NO3)3·6H2O to 0.45, 0.9, and 1.35 g. By comprehensively comparing the emission wavelength and FLQY of the products obtained under different reaction conditions, the optimal reaction conditions for synthesizing Gd-CDs were determined.

NLS was conjugated to the surface carboxyl groups of Gd-CDs using EDC/NHS activation to impart targeting to nucleus capability to the Gd-CDs. Specifically, 200 mg of NLS was uniformly dissolved in 30 mL of PBS (pH = 7.4). Then, 1 mmol of EDC and 1 mmol of NHS were added to the solution, which was subsequently incubated in a 25 °C water bath with constant stirring for 2 h to allow the carboxyl groups to be activated. After that, 200 mg of Gd-CDs were added to the solution, and the reaction was continued at the same temperature for 48 h. Following the reaction, the mixture was dialyzed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h (with the deionized water changed every 8 h) to remove unreacted NLS and excess reactants. Finally, the dialyzed sample was lyophilized to obtain the Gd-CDs-NLS powder for further use.

HBA was modified onto the surface of Gd-CDs-NLS to introduce abundant hydrazide groups. DOX was then loaded via hydrazone bonds. Specifically, 1 mmol of HBA, 2 mmol of EDC, and 2 mmol of NHS were added to 100 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) and dissolved with the aid of sonication to achieve uniform dispersion. The solution was then placed in a 25 °C constant temperature water bath and stirred magnetically for 4 h to activate the HBA. Subsequently, 400 mg of Gd-CDs-NLS powder was added to the mixture, dispersed uniformly, and allowed to react for 48 h. The resulting mixture was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h and then lyophilized to obtain HBA-modified Gd-CDs-NLS (Gd-CDs-NLS-HBA).

The hydrazide groups on Gd-CDs-NLS-HBA were used to form hydrazone bonds with the chemotherapeutic drug DOX, resulting in the drug complex (Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX). The relationship between the material ratio and the drug loading efficiency (DLE, %) was investigated to obtain the optimal drug loading amount. A total of 10 mg of Gd-CDs-NLS-HBA and DOX were used in complexes with different mass ratios (Gd-CDs-NLS-HBA:DOX = 1:1, 1:4, and 4:1). These complexes were dissolved in 10 mL of DMSO with 50 μL of glacial acetic acid added. The mixtures were then incubated in a 25 °C constant-temperature shaker (130 rpm) in the dark for drug loading. After a 72 h reaction, dialysis was performed in 200 mL of deionized water using a 1000 Da dialysis bag. After dialysis, the absorbance of the dialysate was measured at 481 nm. The mass of DOX in the dialysate was determined using a DOX standard curve. The mass of DOX in the dialysis bag was calculated by difference. The DLE was calculated using Equation (1).

Where A(DOX Total) represents the initial total amount of DOX in the reaction system, and A(DOX Free) represents the amount of DOX in the dialysate. Finally, Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX with the highest DLE was selected for synthesizing and characterizing the drug complex for targeting to nucleus.

The CCK-8 assay was used to determine the cell viability of THLE-2 cells and HepG2 cells after incubation with Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS solutions to evaluate their cytotoxicity. First, THLE-2 and HepG2 cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 100 µL per well. After 24 h of incubation at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 cell culture incubator, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS buffer. Then, Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS solutions of varying concentrations (0, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL) were added and incubated for 24 h. Finally, the culture medium and any suspended cells were removed, and the cells were washed again with PBS buffer. A mixed solution (basic culture medium: CCK-8 solution = 9:1) was added, and the cells were incubated in the incubator for 1.5 h. The optical density (OD) of each well was then measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader. The formula for calculating cell viability is as Equation (2).

Hemolysis refers to the ability of a material to break down red blood cells and is an important indicator of a material’s biocompatibility [17]. The hemolysis rate of less than 5 % typically indicates good biocompatibility of the sample. In this study, fresh healthy blood diluted with PBS was repeatedly centrifuged and washed to obtain clean red blood cells. Subsequently, the red blood cell suspension was mixed with Gd-CDs-NLS solutions of varying concentrations and incubated in a 37 °C water bath for 3 h. After further centrifugation, measured the absorbance of the supernatant at 450 nm. Red blood cells mixed with PBS were used as the negative control group, with the hemolysis rate considered to be 0 %, while red blood cells mixed with TX-100 were used as the positive control group, with the hemolysis rate considered to be 100 %.
To investigate the toxicity of Gd-CDs-NLS in vivo, Kunming mice aged 6–8 weeks were selected as the study subjects. Their serum markers, weight changes, and tissue organ toxicity were then examined.

First, mice were injected with either PBS (control group) or a Gd-CDs-NLS solution (4 mg/mL, 100 μL, 10 mg/kg) (experimental group). After being housed in a specific pathogen-free (SPF) barrier environment for 1, 7, and 14 days, blood was collected from the mice. Serum biochemical indices were measured using blood biochemistry analysis kits. Simultaneously, mouse weights were recorded every 2 days, and growth and development were monitored by observing the trend of weight changes. To investigate the tissue and organ toxicity of Gd-CDs-NLS in vivo, mice were injected via the tail vein with either physiological saline (control group) or the Gd-CDs-NLS solution (experimental group). After being housed in a non-SPF environment for 14 days, mice were euthanized using the standard cervical dislocation method. Major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and brain, were collected and processed into hematoxylin-eosin (HE) stained tissue sections. The tissue sections were observed under a microscope to evaluate pathological differences in tissues and organs between the experimental and control groups.

Fluorescence imaging was used to investigate the circulation and metabolism of the Gd-CDs-NLS solution in nude mice. First, 5 mg/mL of Gd-CDs-NLS solution was injected to the nude mice via tail vein. The fluorescence images of the mice were then acquired at different time after injection (0, 1, 6, 8, and 24 h). Subsequently, the mice were euthanized using the standard cervical dislocation method at each of the indicated time. The major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were then harvested and subjected to ex vivo fluorescence imaging to observe the in vivo circulation and metabolism of Gd-CDs-NLS.

Cell co-localization images of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX with DAPI were observed to verify their targeting to nucleus capability, respectively. First, HepG2 cells in the logarithmic growth phase were centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 5 min. The cells were then resuspended in complete culture medium, counted, and adjusted to their density at 1 × 104 cells/mL. The cells were then seeded onto specialized laser confocal culture dishes and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator. When the cell attachment reached 60 %−70 % of the culture dish, the original culture medium was removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS solution. Then, the previously synthesized Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX were co-incubated with the cells for 6 h. After washing three times with PBS, the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 10 − 20 min. The fixative was then removed, and the cells were washed three more times with PBS. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of DAPI was added, and the cells were incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Finally, cell co-localization fluorescence images were observed using a laser confocal microscope at excitation wavelengths of 543, 552, and 500 nm, respectively.

The FL/MRI dual-modal imaging performance of the drug complex for targeting to nucleus were evaluated. First, using H22 tumor-bearing nude mice as a model, the mice were intravenously injected via the tail vein with a Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution (4 mg/mL, 50 μL, 10 mg/kg). After 30 min, the mice were euthanized using the standard cervical dislocation method. The internal organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumors were then dissected, and their ex vivo FL images were acquired. The imaging conditions were excitation wavelength of 500 nm and emission wavelength of 601 nm for Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX.
In addition, Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution (4 mg/mL, 50 μL, 10 mg/kg) was injected into H22 tumor-bearing nude mice via the tail vein. After 30 min, the tumor regions were imaged using a 7 T MRI scanner.

The CCK-8 assay was used to evaluate the effects of free DOX and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX on the viability of HepG2 liver cancer cells, thereby assessing their in vitro antitumor efficacy. Specific testing methods and the calculation of cell viability are detailed in section 2.4.1.

Female Kunming mice, aged 5 − 6 weeks, were selected for the study. All mice were purchased from the Experimental Animal Center of Shanxi Cancer Hospital, license number: SCXK(Jin)2022–0002. The H22 tumor cell line was provided by the Experimental Animal Center of Shanxi Cancer Hospital. Housing conditions: The mice were housed in an SPF-grade animal facility (alternating light/dark cycles) with the temperature and relative humidity controlled at 22.3 °C and 40.3 %, respectively.

Establishment of a subcutaneous tumor-bearing mouse model: A subcutaneous tumor-bearing mouse model was established by subcutaneously injecting H22 murine liver cancer cells. The H22 cell suspension was mixed with basal culture medium. This procedure was repeated for each mouse to ensure uniform injection of the cell suspension. After adjusting the cell density to 1 × 107 cells/mL, 200 µL of the cell suspension was aspirated into an 18-gauge needle and slowly injected into the right forelimb axilla of each mouse. Subcutaneous injections were performed twice a week. Mice were observed for 30 min post-injection to ensure each mouse recovered normally. The long diameter (a) and short diameter (b) of the tumor were measured daily using calipers. Once the tumor volume reached 100 mm3, in vivo antitumor experiments were initiated. The formula for calculating the tumor volume of the tumor-bearing mice is as follows:
(3)

Twelve tumor-bearing mice were divided into three groups to receive tail vein injections, respectively, including: 100 µL of physiological saline, 100 µL of DOX solution (5 mg/kg, 100 µL) of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution with a DOX concentration of 5 mg/kg. Four injections were administered over a 15-day treatment period, with each dose remaining consistent. Tumor volume and mouse weight were measured every three days. At the end of the treatment period, each group of tumor-bearing mice and their tumor tissues were photographed and weighed. A comparison of tumor volume and weight across the three groups directly indicated the antitumor performance of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX.

Major internal organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) from tumor-bearing mice in the physiological saline group and the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX group were collected for HE staining and sectioning, respectively. Analysis of the tissue sections was performed to assess whether Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX caused any adverse effects on the tissues of the tumor-bearing mice.

When a fluorescent probe has a longer emission wavelength, it can avoid autofluorescence interference from biological tissues during imaging. This is crucial for improving image clarity and accuracy [18]. Furthermore, the FLQY, which represents the number of fluorescence photons generated per unit of excitation photons, is another critical parameter for evaluating the performance of Gd-CDs. A high FLQY indicates that it can generate more fluorescence signal, thereby enhancing imaging sensitivity and precision [19]. Therefore, the optimal synthesis conditions for Gd-CDs are selected based on both the emission wavelength and FLQY.

Emission spectra of the products under different reaction conditions were measured at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. S1a − g). The optimal emission wavelengths under the different reaction conditions were: 638, 639, 638, 630, 582, 636, and 637 nm, with FLQY values of 1.38 %, 1.82 %, 1.34 %, 1.12 %, 0.42 %, 0.42 %, and 0.87 %, respectively. When the reaction conditions were 0.9 g of Gd(NO3)3·6H2O reacted at 140 °C for 8 h, the solution exhibited the longest emission wavelength at 639 nm, and the strongest emission intensity of the Gd-CDs was observed at 525 nm (Fig. S1b) with a FLQY value of 1.82 %.

After comprehensively evaluating the emission wavelength and FLQY of Gd-CDs, the optimal reaction conditions were determined to be: 0.76 g of thiourea, 1.92 g of CA, and 0.9 g of Gd(NO3)3·6H2O as reactants, reacted at 140 °C for 8 h. This condition yielded Gd-CDs with optimal emission wavelength and FLQY. The Gd-CD powder synthesized under this condition was used for all subsequent characterization and experiments.

First, the morphology of the Gd-CDs was characterized. The TEM image (Fig. S2a) showed that the Gd-CDs are roughly spherical in shape, with a uniform size distribution, and no significant agglomeration is observed. Statistical analysis of the particle size (Fig. S2b) showed that the average particle size of Gd-CDs is approximately 4.39 nm, exhibiting a normal distribution.

XPS was used to analyze the chemical composition of Gd-CDs. As shown in Fig. S2c, the XPS spectrum of the Gd-CDs has five peaks corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, Gd4d, and Gd3d, confirming the presence of C, O, N, and Gd elements. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S2d) shows four peaks at 284.89, 286.51, 287.99, and 288.90 eV, corresponding to C − O/C − N, C=C, C − C, and C=O bonds, respectively [20–23]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. S2e) has two peaks at 533.53 and 531.99 eV, corresponding to C − O and C=O bonds, respectively [24]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S2f) shows two peaks at 400.02 and 404.90 eV, attributed to pyridinic N and amino N, respectively [25]. As shown in Fig. S2g, the high-resolution Gd4d spectrum shows two absorption peaks at 142.96, 148.38, and 153.32 eV, corresponding to spin–orbit coupled Gd4d5/2 and Gd4d3/2. The high-resolution Gd3d spectrum (Fig. S2h) showed two absorption peaks at 1187.96 and 1219.95 eV, which correspond to Gd3d5/2 and Gd3d3/2, respectively. The positions and intensities of these peaks are consistent with Gd3+ in Gd2O3 [26,27]. The presence of these absorption peaks further confirms that Gd3+ has been incorporated into Gd-CDs. Furthermore, the gadolinium content in the Gd-CDs, as determined by ICP-OES, is 6.1 %.

NLS and HBA were further modified onto the surface of Gd-CDs, and further DOX was loaded. To investigate the impact of the raw material ratio of Gd-CDs-NLS-HBA and DOX on DLE and obtain the optimal material for subsequent experiments, three drug loading ratios (Gd-CDs-NLS-HBA:DOX = 1:4, 1:1, and 4:1) were tested. The corresponding DLEs were 28.8 %, 37.2 %, and 49.3 %, respectively. The highest drug loading efficiency was achieved with the loading ratio of 4:1, and this complex was used for subsequent characterization. FTIR was used to investigate the composition and surface functional groups of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX.

In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 a), the absorption band in the region of 3100–3700 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H [28], and the vibration band in the region of 1500 − 1800 cm−1 corresponds to C=C/C=O [29]. For Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS, the absorption peak observed at 2926 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–H, the absorption peak observed at 1417 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N, and the absorption peak at 1100 cm−1 corresponds to the C–O absorption peak. Compared with Gd-CDs, a new absorption peak appears at 1637 cm−1 for Gd-CDs-NLS and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX, which is a characteristic absorption band of the C=N bond in NLS, thus demonstrating the modification of NLS on the surface of Gd-CDs [30]. For Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX, the hydroxyl absorption peak is intensified, and the absorption peaks in the region of 1000 − 2000 cm−1 are reduced, indicating the loading of DOX on the surface of Gd-CDs-NLS.

The optical properties of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX were characterized using UV–vis and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 1b showed the UV–vis spectra of the Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solutions. As can be seen, the Gd-CDs solution exhibits distinct absorption peaks at 272, 340, and 540 nm. The absorption bands at 272 and 340 nm in the ultraviolet region are attributed to the π-π* transitions of aromatic C=C bonds [31], and the absorption peak around 540 nm in the visible region is a typical absorption band for aromatic structures, indicating a large conjugated structure within the Gd-CDs [11].In the UV–vis spectrum of the Gd-CDs-NLS solution, the absorption peak at 272 nm is inherited from Gd-CDs, while the absorption peak at 540 nm is diminished. The UV–vis spectrum of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX showed a weak absorption peak at 482 nm, corresponding to the absorption peak at 540 nm for Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS. A distinct absorption peak appears at 268 nm, which may be due to the characteristic absorption peak of the grafted DOX [32].

Fig. 1c showed that the optimal excitation wavelengths of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX are 543, 552, and 500 nm, respectively. Fig. 1d − f showed the FL spectra of the three materials, with optimal emission wavelengths at 639, 634, and 601 nm, respectively. It can be observed that as the excitation wavelength is gradually increased, the peak shapes and positions of the three materials remain unchanged. This indicates that they have independent excitation wavelengths and all exhibit red light emission. Furthermore, the measured FLQY values for the three materials are 1.82 %, 2.23 %, and 2.69 %, respectively. After the loading of NLS and DOX, the optimal emission wavelength of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX (601 nm) exhibits a blue shift compared with Gd-CDs (639 nm) under the optimal excitation wavelength. The FLQY increases from 1.82 % to 2.69 %, indicating their potential for FL imaging.

In comparison with the emission wavelength of Gd-CDs from various studies, Gd-CDs obtained in this work show long wavelength emission (as shown in Table 1 ), demonstrating its deeper penetration in bioimaging.

The MRI capabilities of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX were tested on a 7 T magnetic resonance imaging system. A higher longitudinal relaxivity (r1) indicates better imaging performance. As shown in Fig. S3a, the T1-MRI signal intensity of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX, and the control group gadodiamide all show an increasing trend with increasing Gd3+ concentration; the images become brighter, indicating that all three have T1-MRI imaging capabilities. At the same Gd3+ concentration, the T1-MRI signal intensity of both Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX is stronger than that of gadodiamide. The relaxivity of the commercial contrast agent gadodiamide is 2.88 mM−1 s−1, while the relaxivity of Gd-CDs is 15.79 mM−1 s−1, approximately 5 times that of gadodiamide; the relaxivity of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX is 8.25 mM−1 s−1, approximately 3 times that of gadodiamide (Fig. S3b). Compared with the r1 values reported in most studies (as shown in Table 1), Gd-CDs prepared in this study achieved an r1 value of 15.79 mM−1 s−1, which is a significant improvement. This result can likely be attributed to its unique nanostructure, whose larger surface area effectively enhances the interaction between Gd3+ and surrounding water molecules, thereby significantly improving the relaxation efficiency. These results indicate that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX possesses excellent magnetic properties, making it suitable for magnetic resonance imaging.

To realize their application in bioimaging and therapy, the biosafety of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS were evaluated. Their cytotoxicity was investigated firstly. Fig. S4 showed the cell viability of THLE-2 and HepG2 cells after 24 h of incubation with Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4a, the viability of THLE-2 cells remains around 100 % after incubation with Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS. As shown in Fig. S4b, after incubation of HepG2 cells with Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS, cell viability decreases as the solution concentration increases. When the concentrations of the Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS solutions are increased to 100 μg/mL, the viability of HepG2 cells is around 70 % and 80 %, respectively. The above results indicate that both Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS have low cytotoxicity and exhibit good biocompatibility.

The biocompatibility of Gd-CDs-NLS was further investigated by assessing its hemolytic activity. Fig. S4c showed the results of the hemolysis assay with different concentrations of Gd-CDs-NLS. Red blood cells treated with PBS were used as a negative control, with a hemolysis rate considered to be 0, and red blood cells treated with TX-100 were used as a positive control, with a hemolysis rate considered to be 100 %.

As can be seen in the figure, the supernatant of the negative control group is clear and transparent, with a color essentially the same as PBS, and cell precipitates are visible at the bottom of the tube, confirming that no hemolysis occurred. In contrast, the positive control group exhibits a dark red color, with no precipitates at the bottom of the tube, demonstrating that the red blood cells were completely lysed. In the experimental groups, as the concentration of Gd-CDs-NLS increases, the supernatant color showed almost no visible difference, and significant cell precipitates are present at the bottom of the tubes, indicating that no significant hemolysis occurred. Quantitative analysis of the hemolysis rate using UV–Vis yielded the results shown in Fig. S4d. The measured hemolysis rates were 0.08 %, 1.65 %, 3.39 %, 1.04 %, 2.63 %, 2.73 %, and 4.76 %, respectively. All the measured hemolysis rates are less than 5 %, further demonstrating that Gd-CDs-NLS has good hemocompatibility as a carrier [40].

Serum biomarker analysis was used to further characterize the biosafety of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS, including white blood cell count (WBC), hemoglobin (HGB), platelet count (PLT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), blood urea nitrogen (UREA), and creatinine (CREA). Among these, WBC reflects the immune level, HGB is an important indicator for measuring blood’s oxygen-carrying capacity, and PLT’s main functions are coagulation and hemostasis, as well as repairing damaged blood vessels. Serum AST and ALT are indicators of liver function, while UREA and CREA are important indicators for evaluating kidney function.

Fig. S5a − d showed that the HGB levels of Kunming mice treated with the PBS control group and the Gd-CDs-NLS experimental group showed no significant changes in the short or long term after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-NLS. WBC levels initially remained stable after injection of Gd-CDs and then increased, while PLT levels slowly decreased with injection time. In contrast, after injection of the Gd-CDs-NLS solution, both WBC and PLT levels first increased and then decreased with increasing injection time, but remained within normal ranges. CREA levels showed no significant difference after injection of Gd-CDs-NLS, while AST, ALT, and UREA levels gradually increased over time, which may be related to the body’s clearance mechanisms (Fig. S5e − g). Furthermore, all the above indicators remained within normal ranges, indicating that Gd-CDs-NLS has low toxicity in vivo. The body weight growth curve can indirectly reflect the biosafety of the material to some extent. As shown in Fig. S5h, the weight gain trend of the experimental group mice over 14 days was slightly lower than that of the control group. No mouse deaths occurred during the observation period, and the results demonstrate that Gd-CDs-NLS has high in vivo biosafety. A certain volume of physiological saline and Gd-CDs-NLS solution were injected into the tail veins of mice, respectively. After 14 days, the internal organs of the mice were collected and subjected to HE staining. As shown in Fig. S5i, there were no significant differences in the tissue and organ sections between the Gd-CDs-NLS group and the physiological saline group.

To investigate the circulatory and metabolic performance of Gd-CDs-NLS in vivo, a solution with a concentration of 5 mg/mL was injected intravenously into nude mice. The metabolic pathway in vivo was then observed through fluorescence imaging.

As the injection time increased, the fluorescence signal in the nude mice stabilized. The fluorescence was strongest throughout the body, reaching its peak at 1 h post-injection as it circulated with the blood, and then gradually weakened with time while remaining relatively stable. At 24 h post-injection, there was essentially no significant fluorescence signal (Fig. 2 a), indicating that Gd-CDs-NLS can be eliminated from the body within 24 h.

To further investigate the metabolic pathway of Gd-CDs-NLS in nude mice, cervical dislocation was performed on nude mice at different time after injection to harvest major organs including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys. The results showed stronger fluorescence signals in the liver and kidneys, suggesting that Gd-CDs-NLS is excreted via feces and urine (Fig. 2b) [41].

After intravenous tail vein injection, Gd-CDs-NLS enters the bloodstream. Fig. 2c showed the fluorescence intensity histograms in the liver and kidneys of nude mice at different time after injection. Fluorescence signals began to appear in the liver and kidneys at 1 h post-injection, gradually increasing in intensity before decreasing. The highest accumulation of Gd-CDs-NLS, and thus the strongest fluorescence intensity, was observed at 6 h post-injection. With increasing time, Gd-CDs-NLS was eliminated via urine and feces, primarily cleared by the liver and kidneys within 24 h. These observations demonstrate that Gd-CDs-NLS possesses advantages of rapid metabolism and low toxicity.

Following the assessment of biosafety, the nucleus targeting capability of the three materials was analyzed by co-incubating them with HepG2 cells for 6 h and then observing them under a laser confocal microscope. The analysis included: bright field (to observe cell position and morphology), fluorescence field (to visualize the localization of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-NLS, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX within the cells), blue channel (to observe the cell nuclei), and a merge field (to observe co-localization).

The results, shown in Fig. 3 a, reveal that the red fluorescence of Gd-CDs-NLS and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX completely overlaps with the blue fluorescence of DAPI-stained cell nuclei after co-incubation with HepG2 cells.The red fluorescence of Gd-CDs, on the other hand, was mostly located in the cytoplasm and showed minimal overlap with the blue fluorescence of the cell nuclei, indicating that Gd-CDs failed to enter the nucleus. These results demonstrated that Gd-CDs-NLS and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX can enter the nucleus and possess high targeting to nucleus ability.

To verify the FL/MRI dual-modal imaging capability of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX, both in vivo FL imaging and MRI imaging were performed on the tumor region of tumor-bearing mice. First, the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution was injected into the tumor-bearing mice via the tail vein. Fig. 3b showed the ex vivo fluorescence images of the mouse tumor and major organs, indicating that the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution accumulated in the tumor. Simultaneously, relatively bright fluorescence signals were observed in the liver and kidneys, suggesting that the material can be used for in vivo FL imaging and can be metabolized out of the body through the liver and kidneys.

Fig. 3c showed the magnetic resonance images of the tumor-bearing mice, with the tumor region showing bright. This also indicated that the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution can accumulate in the tumor and has a high T1-MRI signal, demonstrating that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX has a strong MRI imaging capability. The above results demonstrate that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX can target tumors precisely and possesses excellent FL/MRI dual-modal imaging performance.

The antitumor properties of the drug complex were studied both in vitro and in vivo. HepG2 cells were used as model cells, and the CCK-8 assay was employed to evaluate the antitumor effect in vitro. After incubating Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution with HepG2 cells for 24 h, when the concentration of the material reached 100 μg/mL, the survival rate of HepG2 cells decreased to approximately 55 %, which was better than that of the free DOX group (Fig. 4 a). This indicates that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX exhibits a significant targeting inhibitory effect on liver cancer cells.

The effect of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX on inhibiting tumor growth in vivo was evaluated using H22 tumor-bearing mice. The H22 tumor-bearing mice were injected with saline (control group), free DOX solution, and Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX solution, respectively. According to the tumor volume and weight shown in Fig. 4b − d, both the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX group and the free DOX group were able to inhibit tumor growth, showing better tumor-inhibiting effects compared with the control group. Moreover, the tumor-inhibiting effect of the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX group was superior to that of the DOX group. It is demonstrated that the excellent inhibitory effect of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX is attributed to the introduction of nucleus targeting molecules.

It can be also observed that the body weight of mice in all three groups increased steadily over time (Fig. 4e), indicating that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX had no significant effect on the body weight of tumor-bearing mice. After treatment, the major internal organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) of mice from the saline control and the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX group were collected, embedded in paraffin, sectioned, and stained with HE for histological examination and analysis.

As shown in Fig. 4f, there were no significant differences in the major internal organs between the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX group and the saline control group, demonstrating that the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX complex can accumulate in tumor tissues, without apparent cellular damagein other tissues. This confirms the effectiveness of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX for precise treatment of liver cancer in vivo.

This study reports a novel magnetic carbon dot drug complex for targeting to nucleus for liver cancer therapy and FL/MRI dual-modal imaging. Gd-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid, neutral red, and thiourea as carbon sources and gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate as the gadolinium source. NLS and DOX were subsequently conjugated to the surface of Gd-CDs, obtaining a Gd-CD drug complex for targeting to nucleus with FL/MRI dual-modal imaging capabilities. This complex exhibited a high drug loading efficiency of 49.3 %. Optical and magnetic characterization revealed optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 500 and 601 nm (red emission), respectively, and the r1 of 8.25 mM−1 s−1, approximately three times that of the commercial contrast agent gadodiamide, demonstrating the FL/MRI dual-modal imaging capability of Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX. Biosafety studies showed that the Gd-CDs-NLS vector exhibited negligible toxicity to THLE-2 and HepG2 cells. Hemolysis assays demonstrated its good hemocompatibility. Serum biomarkers in Kunming mice injected with Gd-CDs-NLS remained within normal ranges, and no abnormalities were observed in major organ tissue sections. The rapid metabolism of Gd-CDs-NLS in mice (within 24 h) further indicated good biocompatibility. Targeting to nucleus imaging experiments showed that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX rapidly entered cells and localized to the nucleus, indicating strong targeting to nucleus ability. In vivo FL and MRI imaging experiments in H22 tumor-bearing mice demonstrated that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX accumulated in the tumor region and exhibited excellent FL/MRI dual-modal imaging capabilities. Antitumor efficacy studies showed that Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX exhibited good tumor-suppressive effects in both HepG2 cells and tumor-bearing mice, suggesting its broad application prospects in liver cancer therapy.

This research provided an initial exploration of the synthesis and dual-modal imaging and therapeutic capabilities of the Gd-CDs-NLS-DOX complex, and showed its feasibility and effectiveness. However, numerous challenges must be addressed before this system can be translated to human liver cancer treatment. Although no apparent toxicity has been observed over a 15-day period, long-term toxicity requires further in vitro and in vivo study. Furthermore, this study investigated the therapeutic effect of the complex in HepG2 cells and H22 tumor-bearing mice, and future studies will include a broader range of human liver cancer cell lines and relevant animal models. Future study will also focus on optimizing the injection dosage and elucidating potential drug resistance mechanisms to maximize therapeutic efficacy.
